strands.title: Replay Module Requirements & Design Intent
strands.content: The user is requesting a replay module (src/pichay/replay.py) that reconstructs offline simulations of context paging behavior. The core purpose is to enable analysis of paging effectiveness by replaying the exact message array states as they existed at each API call turn, then running compaction logic to detect page faults (when the model re-requests evicted content) and computing aggregate statistics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage). This is a post-hoc evaluation tool that bridges proxy logs to the pager compaction logic.

key_claims.text: The replay module must parse proxy JSONL logs (format from proxy.py) to reconstruct paging scenarios
The module must reconstruct the messages array as it would have existed at each API call turn
Page faults occur when the model's actual next action re-requests content that was evicted
The module must provide per-session and aggregate statistics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction %)
A CLI entry point with argparse is required with --age-threshold, --min-size, and --json flags

strands.title: Code Context & Existing Patterns
strands.content: The assistant has begun reading the codebase (proxy.py, pager.py, eval.py) to understand existing patterns, data structures, and logging format. The user explicitly mandated studying existing code thoroughly before writing: 'Read the existing pager.py, proxy.py, and eval.py thoroughly before writing any code. Match the coding style and patterns.' This establishes that the new replay module must integrate cohesively with the existing architecture. The JSONL log format from proxy.py and the compaction logic from pager.py are the key dependencies for understanding implementation.

key_claims.text: The pager.py contains compaction logic that can be applied to reconstructed message snapshots
The proxy.py logs request/response metrics in JSONL format with optional context paging
eval.py provides existing evaluation patterns that the replay module should follow or adapt

strands.title: Critical Implementation Unknowns
strands.content: The assistant has not yet completed reading the source files, so the actual structure of JSONL logs, the precise API of pager.py's compaction logic, and the specific metrics computed by eval.py remain partially unknown. The exact format of the messages array at each turn, how page faults are identified, and how 'bytes saved' and 'reduction percentage' are calculated have not been derived from the code yet.

key_claims.text: The JSONL log format and which fields are available for reconstruction is not yet known
The pager.py API (function signatures, return types) needs to be understood before implementation
The definition of 'page fault' in practice (how to detect re-requested evicted content) is unclear

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Full content of proxy.py, pager.py, and eval.py source files
declared_losses.why: The tool_result in user turn 5 only shows the first few lines of one file (proxy.py), truncated at line 5. The assistant's read operations (turns 3-4) are placeholders without actual output.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Concrete data structure details: JSONL schema, message array format, pager API
declared_losses.why: Without full file content, specific field names, function signatures, and data shapes cannot be determined. This is essential for the next implementation cycle.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Semantic understanding of how the existing codebase implements compaction and fault detection
declared_losses.why: Partial file content prevents inference of the full paging logic and how faults are conceptually detected

open_questions: What is the exact JSONL schema for proxy.py logs? Which fields capture the messages array, API calls, and model responses?
What is the API of the pager.py compaction logic? What functions are exported and what do they return?
How is a 'page fault' operationally detected? Is it identified by comparing requested content against evicted records?
What metrics does eval.py compute, and should the replay module follow its statistical computation patterns?
How should 'bytes saved' and 'reduction percentage' be calculated from paging operations?
Should the replay module reconstruct the full conversation turn-by-turn, or work with aggregated snapshots?
What coding style (naming conventions, error handling, documentation) does the existing codebase use?
